module_map.responsibility: Core framework including request/response handling, middleware pipeline orchestration, and base handler infrastructure
Request handling infrastructure including base handler implementations for WSGI/ASGI, middleware loading and execution pipeline
URL routing and resolution system that maps HTTP requests to view functions via URL patterns and resolvers
HTTP layer defining request/response objects, query dictionaries, and multipart parsing for handling incoming/outgoing HTTP traffic
View layer providing base view classes and generic views for processing HTTP requests and returning responses
Generic view classes implementing common web patterns (CRUD operations, form handling) as reusable components
ORM layer defining models, managers, query sets, and database operations for CRUD functionality and data persistence
Database layer providing connection management, transaction handling, and database router infrastructure
Middleware pipeline providing cross-cutting concerns (CSRF protection, session handling, security headers) executed around view processing
Configuration system handling Django settings with lazy evaluation and centralized configuration management
Template rendering system providing context processors, template engines, and response rendering for generating HTML/output

pipeline_trace.description: ASGI/WSGI handler receives incoming HTTP request, instantiates HttpRequest object with headers, body, and metadata. Validates host, decodes query parameters, and initializes middleware pipeline.
URL resolver matches request path against URL patterns using ResolverMatch. Resolves view function/callable and captures named URL parameters. Sets up URLconf for the request lifecycle.

Middleware classes process request in LIFO order using process_request() hooks. Each middleware can modify request, perform authentication, log, or short-circuit the pipeline. CSRF middleware validates tokens here.

View.as_view() converts class-based views to callable functions. Dispatch method selects appropriate HTTP method handler (get/post/etc.). URL parameters are bound to view function kwargs. Initialize response object and context.

View functions perform business logic: retrieve/persist data via ORM, process forms, call business services. May render templates, return static content, or redirect. Uses QuerySet operations for database access.

QuerySet methods execute ORM queries against database backend. Includes filtering, ordering, aggregation, and joins. Database router selects appropriate database connection. Queries return QuerySet iterators that lazily execute SQL when consumed.
TemplateResponse renders templates with context data. Can use Django template engine or pluggable template backends. Generates final HTTP response object with status code, headers, and body content.

Middleware classes process response in FIFO order using process_response() hooks. Add headers, modify content, perform cleanup, log metrics. Final middleware closes database connections and signals request completion.
Handler transmits final HttpResponse object back through ASGI/WSGI server to client. Ensures proper HTTP headers and status codes. Cleanup of request-specific state.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Central settings/configuration hub read by nearly all Django subsystems. Settings object provides global configuration needed by ORM, middleware, views, templates, and utilities. Configures database connections, authentication backends, template engines, and third-party integrations.

Middleware orchestration hub that coordinates the entire request/response pipeline. Controls middleware loading (load_middleware), execution order (_get_response), and exception handling (process_exception_by_middleware). Serves as the anchor for all cross-cutting concerns.

Cross-cutting concerns hub implementing authentication (SessionMiddleware), security (CSRF), performance (cache), and other middleware patterns. Every HTTP request passes through middleware pipeline, making it essential for dependency injection of common services.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Async-compatible structured logging middleware that captures per-request metadata (view name, ORM query count, response time) and emits structured log entries for observability and debugging.

new_types.name: StructuredLoggingMiddleware
new_types.description: Middleware class with async-compatible process_request/process_response hooks that captures timing, view identification, and database query metrics

new_types.name: RequestContextLogger
new_types.description: Context manager or decorator for view functions to track per-request ORM operations and timing

new_types.name: RequestMetrics
new_types.description: Data structure to store request-level metrics (start_time, query_count, view_name, status_code)

change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Standard Django middleware pattern using MiddlewareMixin base class with process_request() and process_response() methods. Follow existing middleware like CsrfViewMiddleware and SessionMiddleware in structure and documentation patterns.
change_proposal.integration_point: Middleware is injected at WSGI/ASGI handler startup via load_middleware() function in BaseHandler.load_middleware(). Register in MIDDLEWARE setting. Follows Django's middleware pipeline pattern with configurable execution order.
risks: Performance overhead from query counting and timing instrumentation — must be opt-in via setting to avoid production impact
Thread/async-safety of metrics collection — must use thread-local or ASGI contexts to avoid race conditions in concurrent requests
Log volume explosion — structured logging can generate large volumes of log data; needs log rotation and sampling configuration
Initialization order — middleware must be registered after transaction middleware and database middleware to access accurate query counts
